target.name: (+)-cylindricine B
target.motivation: Marine natural products are a unique source of leads for small molecule therapeutics, but their sourcing in large quantities is challenging. Synthetic chemistry plays an elevated role in elucidating their medicinal indices. The cylindricine family is structurally intricate and stereochemically dense, with a primary synthetic hurdle being the chiral alpha-tertiary amine moiety. This work reports the first enantioselective synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B.

success_criteria.free_text: First enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B. Concise synthesis (5 or 6 steps) from commercially available starting materials.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-Cl bond formation and C-N bond formation
key_disconnections.reasoning: The chlorinated piperidine moiety could be forged at a late stage in the synthesis between the piperidine nitrogen and the adventitious butenyl side chain.
Intramolecular enolate trapping via nucleophilic substitution of a pendant halide would enable the generation of the cis-azadecalinone ring system.
Introduction of the butenyl group would require the addition of a four-carbon nucleophile in conjugate fashion to a vinylogous amide, establishing a fully substituted alpha-tertiary amine center.
Vinylogous amide was envisioned to arise from in situ activation and dearomatization of commercial pyridine with an anion following formation of an intermediate pyridinium.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis hinged on dearomative retrosynthetic logic combined with a tactical advance in the generation of congested, cyclic, alpha-tertiary amine centers. This approach aimed to solve the difficult tactical requirement of forming the alpha-tertiary amine, enabling concise synthetic access through feedstock pyridine and simple organometallic nucleophilic coupling partners.

thought.background: The cylindricine natural product family is characterized by a tricyclic scaffold with four stereogenic centres, a piperidone core, and a C14 heteroatomic functionality. Each family member has a chiral alpha-tertiary amine moiety that constitutes the primary synthetic hurdle. Many racemic and enantiospecific preparations have been reported, but only one approach towards cylindricine C is enantioselective.
thought.gap: No enantioselective synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B has been reported. The establishment of the desired alpha tertiary amine either required circuitous downstream functional group interconversion or resulted in an undesired and/or unresolvable stereochemical outcome in previous dearomative strategies.
thought.decision: To achieve the first enantioselective synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B, a dearomative retrosynthetic logic will be employed, focusing on a tactical advance in generating congested, cyclic, alpha-tertiary amine centers. This will involve a late-stage chlorination, intramolecular cyclization, conjugate addition to a vinylogous amide, and dearomatization of a pyridine.

evidence.text_span: Herein, we report the inaugural enantioselective preparation of (+)-cylindricine B (9), a flagship isomer of this marine alkaloid family. A retrosynthetic analysis of cylindricine B (9) is depicted in Fig. 1B. It was envisioned that the chlorinated piperidine moiety could be forged at a late stage in the synthesis between the piperidine nitrogen and the adventitious butenyl side chain (see 10). Furthermore, intramolecular enolate trapping via nucleophilic substitution of a pendant halide would enable the generation of the cis-azadecalinone ring system. The planned introduction of the butenyl group in 10 would require the addition of a four-carbon nucleophile (12) in conjugate fashion to vinylogous amide 11. This prospective transformation is deceptively challenging, and while this type of addition is common for unsubstituted variants, the establishment of this fully substituted centre has little precedent in the chemical literature. However, we envisioned that its realization in this synthetic context would enable a direct access to the appropriately functionalized piperidone core. Vinylogous amide 11 was envisioned to arise from in situ activation and dearomatization of commercial pyridine 13 with anion 14 following formation of an intermediate pyridinium. It should be noted that Donohoe and Comins have described dearomative strategies towards the cylindricines. But, as articulated in their work, the establishment of the desired alpha tertiary amine either required circuitous downstream functional group interconversion or resulted in an undesired and/or unresolvable stereochemical outcome. By solving this difficult tactical requirement (i.e. conversion of 11 to 10), concise synthetic access would be realized through the leveraging of a feedstock pyridine and simple organometallic nucleophilic coupling partners as starting materials.

thought.background: An initial racemic construction was evaluated to assess feasibility and selectivity of each step. The first step involves the dearomatization of a pyridine.
thought.gap: The feasibility and selectivity of the dearomatization and subsequent steps need to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To form piperidinone 17, commercially available pyridine 15 will be treated with methyl chloroformate followed by n-hexylmagnesium bromide (16) at -78 °C.

evidence.text_span: Commercially available pyridine 15 was selected as the starting point, and was treated with methyl chloroformate followed by n-hexylmagnesium bromide (16) at -78 °C to give piperidinone 17 in 84% yield.

thought.background: Piperidinone 17 has been successfully synthesized. The next key step is the construction of the fully substituted center via conjugate addition.
thought.gap: The conditions for the conjugate addition to establish the fully substituted center need to be optimized, and its diastereoselectivity needs to be determined.
thought.decision: To form 19a, 17 will be reacted with butenylmagnesium bromide (18) in the presence of superstoichiometric amounts of organocuprate and excess trimethylsilyl triflate (TMSOTf), followed by acidic hydrolysis.

outcome.observation: Desired product 19a was obtained in 67% yield after acidic hydrolysis of the intermediate silyl enol ether. Catalytic amounts of Cu resulted in lower yields and longer reaction times.

evidence.text_span: Next, the key construction of the fully substituted centre was evaluated. While many reaction parameters (e.g., metal salts, Lewis acids) and substrates (differing carbamoyl groups) were evaluated, it was determined that the use of superstoichiometric amounts of organocuprate in the presence of excess trimethylsilyl triflate (TMSOTf) resulted in a 67% yield of desired product 19a (see ESI†), following acidic hydrolysis of the intermediate silyl enol ether. Catalytic amounts (20 mol%) of Cu can be employed, but with lower yields and longer reaction times.

thought.background: Product 19a was obtained from the conjugate addition. The diastereoselectivity of this addition is crucial for the synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B.
thought.gap: 2D NMR spectroscopic analysis was inconclusive regarding the facial preference of the addition. The diastereoselectivity needs to be validated.
thought.decision: To validate the diastereoselectivity, 19a will be converted to a more elaborated precursor (22b) whose structure has been previously reported by Snider and coworkers for comparison.

outcome.observation: 2D NMR was inconclusive. The strategy is to synthesize a known intermediate (22b) for comparison.

evidence.text_span: At this stage of the synthesis, however, it was unknown whether this addition gave the desired diastereoselectivity needed to complete the synthesis of 9. Thus, as 2D NMR spectroscopic analysis was inconclusive as to the facial preference of the addition, it was decided to validate this through the synthesis of a more elaborated and putative precursor. In this vein, it was seen that targeting 22b (see Fig. 2) was operative, as its structure, if correct, had been previously reported by Snider and coworkers.

thought.background: To validate the diastereoselectivity of the conjugate addition, 19a needs to be converted to 22b. This involves converting the chloride to an iodide, followed by intramolecular cyclization.
thought.gap: The conversion of 19a to 20 and then to 21a needs to be performed.
thought.decision: To form 21a, chloropiperidine 19a will be converted to its corresponding iodide (20) with NaI, and then treated with KOtBu in THF to promote cyclization to the cis-azadecalinone 21a.

outcome.observation: Iodide 20 was formed, which then cyclized to cis-azadecalinone 21a, establishing the third stereocentre at C5, in 65% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: In this vein, it was seen that targeting 22b (see Fig. 2) was operative, as its structure, if correct, had been previously reported by Snider and coworkers. With this goal in mind, chloropiperidine 19a was converted to its corresponding iodide (20) with NaI, and then treated with KOtBu in THF to promote cyclization to the cis-azadecalinone 21a, while establishing the third stereocentre at C5 in 65% over the two steps.

thought.background: Azadecalinone 21a has been synthesized. The next step is to cleave the methyl carbamate to obtain aminoketone 22a for comparison with Snider's reported intermediate 22b.
thought.gap: The carbamate cleavage needs to be performed.
thought.decision: To form 22a, the methyl carbamate of 21a will be cleaved with 2-mercaptoethanol in the presence of potassium phosphate at elevated temperature.

outcome.observation: Aminoketone 22a was cleanly delivered in 53% yield.

evidence.text_span: Cleavage of the methyl carbamate was accomplished with 2-mercaptoethanol in the presence of potassium phosphate at elevated temperature to cleanly deliver aminoketone 22a in 53% yield.

thought.background: Aminoketone 22a has been synthesized. This compound was targeted to validate the diastereoselectivity of the earlier conjugate addition by comparing it to Snider's reported intermediate 22b.
thought.gap: The spectroscopic comparison between 22a and 22b needs to be performed.
thought.decision: To determine the diastereoselectivity, 22a will be spectroscopically compared (1H NMR) to Snider's reported intermediate 22b.

outcome.observation: Compound 22a did not spectroscopically (1H NMR) match Snider's reported intermediate (22b). This indicates that the conversion of 17 to 19a occurred in an anti fashion with respect to the established C2 stereocentre, which is significant as it provides evidence that conjugate additions to 2,6-disubstituted 4-piperidinones can proceed with opposite facial preference compared to 2-monosubstituted counterparts.

evidence.text_span: This compound did not spectroscopically (1H NMR) match Snider's reported intermediate (22b), likely indicating that the conversion of 17 to 19a occurred in an anti fashion with respect to the established C2 stereocentre. This anti diastereoselectivity was significant because it provided evidence that conjugate additions to 2,6-disubstituted 4-piperidinones can proceed with the opposite facial preference compared with their 2-monosubstituted counterparts.

thought.background: The diastereoselective nature of the 1,4-addition to 17 has been understood (anti-selectivity). This understanding is crucial for the overall synthetic strategy.
thought.gap: The scope of nucleophiles competent for addition to this prochiral center needs to be further evaluated to demonstrate broader synthetic utility.
thought.decision: To evaluate the scope, various alkyl Grignard reagents (methyl, isopropyl, t-butyl, 2-bromohexane, acetal-bearing, benzyl, vinyl, isopropenyl, phenyl) will be tested in the 1,4-addition to 4-piperidinone 17.

outcome.observation: Various Grignard reagents (methyl, isopropyl, t-butyl, 2-bromohexane, acetal-bearing, benzyl, vinyl, isopropenyl, phenyl) successfully added diastereoselectively to the 4-piperidinone scaffold, yielding products 19b-j with complete diastereocontrol (>20:1). This provides a facile avenue for a diverse array of highly substituted 4-piperidone congeners.

evidence.text_span: Given the success of this diastereoselective 1,4 addition to 17, we wanted to further evaluate the scope of nucleophiles competent for addition to this prochiral centre (see Fig. 3). In addition to butenylmagnesium bromide (16), other alkyl Grignard reagents added diastereoselectively to the 4-piperidinone including methyl (19b), isopropyl (19c), and even tbutylmagnesium bromide (19d) added in with moderate efficiency (22% yield). Utilization of a prochiral Grignard reagent derived from 2-bromohexane resulted in the formation of two new stereocentres with a 2.3:1 diastereomeric ratio about the C7 stereocentre and >20:1 about the C6 stereocentre (see 19e). Another Grignard bearing an acetal functionality (19f), potentially useful for downstream manipulation, was diastereoselectively appended in 65% yield. Benzylmagnesium bromide (19g), vinylmagnesium bromide (19h), and isopropenylmagnesium bromide (19i) were also successfully added onto the piperidone scaffold in 65%, 43%, and 55% yields respectively. Furthermore, product 19j was synthesized in 79% from the addition of the corresponding phenyl Grignard reagent. Importantly, all these additions proceeded with complete diastereocontrol (>20:1) and provide a facile avenue for a diverse array of highly substituted 4-piperidone congeners.

thought.background: The initial racemic approach confirmed the anti-diastereoselectivity of the 1,4-addition. This understanding allows for a modified, enantioselective approach.
thought.gap: The initial strategy was racemic. An enantioselective route is needed for the total synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B.
thought.decision: To achieve an enantioselective synthesis, butenylpyridine 23 will be used as the starting material for the enantioselective generation of piperidinone 24, based on precedent from Harutyunyan and coworkers.

outcome.observation: A modified, enantioselective approach was undertaken, starting with butenylpyridine 23.

evidence.text_span: Having understood the diastereoselective nature for synthesizing this fully substituted centre, a modified approach to the synthesis of cylindricine B (9) was undertaken (Fig. 4). In addition to a strategic change in the starting material, it was also surmised that the synthesis could be rendered enantioselective. To this end, butenylpyridine 23 was employed as the starting material for the enantioselective generation of piperidinone 24.

thought.background: Butenylpyridine 23 is the starting material for the enantioselective generation of piperidinone 24. Harutyunyan's precedent suggests a Cu-catalyzed enantioselective dearomatization.
thought.gap: Optimizing conditions for high enantioselectivity and yield in the dearomatization of 23 to 24.
thought.decision: To form 24, 23 will be treated with 3 equivalents of methyl chloroformate and Grignard 16 (3 equiv.) in the presence of CuBr complexed with (R,R)-Ph-BPE in CH2Cl2.

outcome.observation: Piperidinone 24 was obtained in 67% yield and with 80.3% ee. The enantiomeric excess was lower than desired, attributed to a slightly higher rate for the racemic background reaction.

evidence.text_span: Based on precedent from Harutyunyan and coworkers, treatment of 23 with 3 equivalents of methyl chloroformate and Grignard 16 (3 equiv.) in the presence of the CuBr complexed with (R,R)-Ph-BPE in CH2Cl2 gave 24 in 67% yield and with 80.3% ee. Notably, a higher asymmetric induction was observed when only 2 equivalents of the chloroformate and the nucleophile (16) were employed, but the yield of 24 decreased to only 32%, but with higher enantioselectivity (91.4% ee). We attribute the lower enantiomeric excess in the first iteration was due to a slightly higher rate for the racemic background reaction of the Grignard reagent adding the pyridinium electrophile.

thought.background: The previous attempt to synthesize 24 resulted in 80.3% ee, which is lower than desired due to a racemic background reaction.
thought.gap: Higher enantioselectivity is needed for the synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B.
thought.decision: To improve enantioselectivity, the reaction will be performed with only 2 equivalents of methyl chloroformate and 2 equivalents of Grignard 16. Additionally, the preparation of 16 in ether will be essential for enantioselectivity, avoiding THF as a cosolvent.

outcome.observation: Higher asymmetric induction (91.4% ee) was observed, although the yield decreased to 32%. Preparation of Grignard 16 in ether was essential, as THF as a cosolvent resulted in poor asymmetric induction (17% ee).

evidence.text_span: Notably, a higher asymmetric induction was observed when only 2 equivalents of the chloroformate and the nucleophile (16) were employed, but the yield of 24 decreased to only 32%, but with higher enantioselectivity (91.4% ee). We attribute the lower enantiomeric excess in the first iteration was due to a slightly higher rate for the racemic background reaction of the Grignard reagent adding the pyridinium electrophile. Additionally, the preparation of 16 in ether was essential for the enantioselectivity as utilizing THF as a cosolvent resulted in poor asymmetric induction (17% ee).

thought.background: Enantiomerically enriched piperidinone 24 is in hand. The next step is the diastereoselective addition of a chlorobutyl Grignard to form 19k.
thought.gap: The conditions for this diastereoselective addition need to be applied.
thought.decision: To form 19k, 24 will undergo diastereoselective addition of chlorobutyl Grignard 25 in the presence of CuBr(DMS).

outcome.observation: Piperidone 19k was generated in good yield (55%) and with high diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: With 24 in hand, diastereoselective addition of chlorobutyl Grignard 25 in the presence of CuBr(DMS) generated piperidone 19k in good yield and with high diastereoselectivity.

thought.background: Piperidone 19k has been synthesized. The next step is the intramolecular cyclization to form the azadecalinone 21b.
thought.gap: The cyclization needs to be performed.
thought.decision: To form 21b, 19k will be deprotonated with excess KOtBu to facilitate the intramolecular displacement of the pendant chloride.

outcome.observation: Azadecalinone 21b was formed. This step was separated from the carbamate cleavage to improve overall yield.

evidence.text_span: Next, deprotonation of 19k with excess KOtBu facilitated the intramolecular displacement of the pendant chloride to synthesize the azadecalinone 21b, which, upon further treatment with 2-mercaptoethanol and base gave a modest yield (28%) of amine 22b. Conveniently, separating the cyclization and carbamate cleavage events resulted in a much higher two-step yield of 66% percent (72% and 91% for each step, respectively).

thought.background: Azadecalinone 21b has been synthesized. The next step is the carbamate cleavage to obtain amine 22b.
thought.gap: The carbamate cleavage needs to be performed.
thought.decision: To form 22b, 21b will be treated with 2-mercaptoethanol and base.

outcome.observation: Amine 22b was obtained. Separating this step from the cyclization resulted in a 91% yield for this step.

evidence.text_span: Next, deprotonation of 19k with excess KOtBu facilitated the intramolecular displacement of the pendant chloride to synthesize the azadecalinone 21b, which, upon further treatment with 2-mercaptoethanol and base gave a modest yield (28%) of amine 22b. Conveniently, separating the cyclization and carbamate cleavage events resulted in a much higher two-step yield of 66% percent (72% and 91% for each step, respectively).

thought.background: Amine 22b has been synthesized. The final steps involve chlorination of the amine and aminochlorination of the butenyl side chain to form (+)-cylindricine B (9).
thought.gap: The chlorination and aminochlorination steps need to be performed, and the diastereoselectivity of the aminochlorination needs to be addressed.
thought.decision: To form 9, the amine of 22b will be chlorinated with NCS, and subsequently treated with a CuCl/CuCl2 mixture to promote aminochlorination of the butenyl side chain.

outcome.observation: Chlorination of the amine was cleanly accomplished. Subsequent aminochlorination of the butenyl side chain formed a mixture of cylindricine A, cylindricine B, and their corresponding epimers. Chromatographic separation yielded pure (+)-cylindricine B (9) and a 9:1 mixture of 13-epi-cylindricine B and 13-epi-cylindricine A. The successful synthesis confirmed the diastereoselectivity of the key 1,4-addition.

evidence.text_span: Finally, chlorination of the amine was cleanly accomplished with NCS, and subsequent treatment with a CuCl/CuCl2 mixture promoted amino chlorination of the butenyl side chain. Initially, this reaction formed a mixture of cylindricine A, cylindricine B, and their corresponding epimers. As precedented by Snider and Heathcock, this atom-transfer reaction was not diastereoselective, to which, following chromatographic separation of the reaction mixture, rendered pure samples of (+)-cylindricine B (9) and a 9:1 mixture of 13-epi-cylindricine B and 13-epi-cylindricine A. The successful synthesis of the natural product further confirmed the diastereoselectivity of the key 1,4-addition to establish the chiral alpha-tertiary amine centre.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Maximum overall yield for the enantioselective route.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The synthesis proceeds in 5 or 6 steps from commercially available starting materials.

validation.significance: This is the first reported enantioselective synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B and only the second enantioselective route to the cylindricine family. It demonstrates a rare but tactically important 1,4-addition to a 4-piperidinone and provides a concise route with a maximum 16% overall yield. The work overcomes tactical challenges in constructing multiply substituted piperidines and strategically leverages robust heteroaromatic starting materials, highlighting the potential of dearomative retrosynthetic logic in alkaloid synthesis.

source.title: Enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-cylindricine B.
source.authors: Dukes DM, Atanassov VK, Smith JM.
